network.overall_strategy: This network integrates 10 distinct prebiotic pathways to glycine across three environments: Hydrothermal (iron-catalyzed reductive amination, thermal decomposition of higher amino acids, peracetic acid amination), Surface (Strecker/cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis, UV photoredox routes via glyoxylate), and Biochemical (downstream peptide assembly). The network is anchored by five hub molecules — glyoxylate, formaldehyde, ammonia, acetate, and glycine itself — each participating in ≥3 reactions. Glyoxylate serves as the principal convergence point: four independent upstream reactions feed it (isocitrate cleavage, UV dehydration of dihydroxyacetic acid, glycolic acid photooxidation, and glycolic acid itself from Cannizzaro/photoredox), and it is reductively aminated to glycine by iron catalysis. Glycolic acid is NOT a dead-end: it is explicitly connected to glyoxylate via UV photooxidation (Liu et al., 2021). All orphan molecules from previous attempts are addressed: hydroxylamine is synthesized from NO reduction by Fe⁰, serine has a non-circular upstream (Strecker from glycolaldehyde), and threonine/asparagine are marked as exogenous starting materials from meteoritic delivery (Pizzarello et al., 2006). Stoichiometric errors are corrected: asparagine yields 2 glycine, Cannizzaro requires 2 formaldehyde. Every hydrothermal and surface reaction specifies mineral agents.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2 reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis; serpentinization-derived H2

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is first reduced to CO on magnetite surfaces via reverse water-gas shift, then CO is further hydrogenated to formaldehyde (HCHO) as an early intermediate. Magnetite provides catalytic Fe sites analogous to industrial Fischer-Tropsch catalysts.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of C1 oxygenates from CO2 + H2 on magnetite surfaces is a well-established hydrothermal vent reaction. Formaldehyde is a key early product before further reduction to methanol or higher hydrocarbons. Supported by McCollom et al. (1999) and Holm & Neubeck (2009) who demonstrated organic synthesis on magnetite under hydrothermal conditions.

reactions.name: CO2 reduction to acetate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions; pH 9-11; analog of Wood-Ljungdahl pathway

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is first reduced to formate on greigite surfaces (analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters), then further reduced and coupled to a second CO2/CO-derived C1 unit via an acetyl-CoA pathway analog to yield acetate. Greigite's structural similarity to biological iron-sulfur clusters enables this two-carbon fixation.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to acetate is the prebiotic analog of the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway, first proposed by Russell & Martin (2004) and experimentally supported by Preiner et al. (2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution), who showed H2-dependent CO2 fixation to formate and acetate on FeNiS minerals under alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions.

reactions.name: Strecker condensation to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic surface pool conditions; formaldehyde + HCN + NH3 in evaporitic setting

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia condenses with formaldehyde to form an imine (methyleneimine, H2C=NH), which then undergoes nucleophilic addition by cyanide (CN⁻) to yield aminoacetonitrile (H2N-CH2-CN). This is the classic Strecker aminonitrile formation — the first step of the Strecker amino acid synthesis. Clay surfaces concentrate the reactants and catalyze the condensation.
reactions.reasoning: Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from formaldehyde, HCN, and NH3 is a cornerstone of prebiotic amino acid chemistry, demonstrated in the context of cyanosulfidic protometabolism by Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). Montmorillonite clay catalysis of Strecker-type condensations is supported by Ferris et al. (1996) and Orgel (1998).

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis to glycine (cyanosulfidic)

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic pool conditions; wet-dry cycling promotes hydrolysis; 2 equivalents of H2O consumed

reactions.mechanism: Water attacks the nitrile carbon of aminoacetonitrile, forming 2-aminoacetamide as an intermediate (addition of first H2O). The amide then undergoes further hydrolysis (second H2O) to yield glycine (H2N-CH2-COOH) with release of ammonia (NH3). The net reaction: H2N-CH2-CN + 2H2O → H2N-CH2-COOH + NH3. Sulfide mineral surfaces may assist the hydrolysis by Lewis acid activation of the nitrile group.
reactions.reasoning: Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis to glycine is the final step of the Strecker synthesis pathway, demonstrated under cyanosulfidic conditions by Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). The regeneration of NH3 as a co-product allows catalytic cycling. This is part of the unified cyanosulfidic chemistry that also produces RNA and lipid precursors.

reactions.name: Iron-catalyzed reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent mixing zone; anoxic; pH ~9; 2 equivalents of H2O produced

reactions.mechanism: Hydroxylamine (NH2OH) acts as the nitrogen donor, condensing with the aldehyde group of glyoxylate to form an oxime intermediate (HO-N=CH-COOH). Iron species (Fe⁰ as reductant, Fe²⁺ as Lewis acid catalyst) then reduce the oxime to the amine, yielding glycine (H2N-CH2-COOH) with loss of water. This is analogous to the biological transamination mechanism but uses hydroxylamine instead of an amino acid nitrogen donor.
reactions.reasoning: Directly demonstrated by Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) at 70°C with Fe⁰ and Fe²⁺ in water. This iron-promoted reductive amination is part of a broader proto-metabolic network that interconverts α-keto acids and α-amino acids, establishing a prebiotic analog of core biological metabolism.

reactions.name: Iron-catalyzed retro-aldol cleavage of isocitrate to glyoxylate

conditions.other: Anoxic conditions; analog of isocitrate lyase reaction

reactions.mechanism: Fe²⁺ coordinates the hydroxyl and carboxylate groups of isocitrate, acting as a Lewis acid to facilitate retro-aldol C–C bond cleavage. The C6 tricarboxylic acid is split into glyoxylate (C2, the α-keto acid) and succinate (C4, the dicarboxylic acid). This is the prebiotic analog of the isocitrate lyase reaction found in the glyoxylate shunt of biological metabolism.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) as part of an iron-catalyzed proto-metabolic network at 70°C. Isocitrate is a TCA cycle intermediate generated by the same iron-driven network. Fe²⁺ is abundant in anoxic early Earth hydrothermal systems from serpentinization.

reactions.name: UV-driven dehydration of 2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pool; no ozone shielding on early Earth

reactions.mechanism: UV photons drive the dehydration of the gem-diol form of glyoxylic acid hydrate (2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid, HO-CH(OH)-COOH) to regenerate the aldehyde form (glyoxylate, OHC-COOH) with loss of H2O. TiO2 mineral surfaces may act as photocatalytic sensitizers enhancing the photoredox process.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Liu, Wu, Kufner, Sasselov, Fischer, and Sutherland (2021, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00789-w) as part of a UV-driven photoredox network that produces key metabolic intermediates. The intense UV flux on the early Earth (lacking ozone shielding) provides the driving force for this photochemical dehydration.

reactions.name: UV photooxidation of glycolic acid to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pool; photooxidation of alcohol to aldehyde

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photooxidation converts the α-hydroxyl group of glycolic acid (HO-CH2-COOH) to the aldehyde of glyoxylate (OHC-COOH). TiO2 mineral surfaces generate reactive oxygen intermediates under UV, which abstract hydrogen from the C–H bond adjacent to the hydroxyl, followed by loss of a second hydrogen to form the carbonyl. This is the reverse of the reduction observed by Muchowska et al. (2019) and Ritson (2021).
reactions.reasoning: The interconversion between glycolic acid and glyoxylate is documented in both directions. Reduction (glyoxylate → glycolic acid) was shown by Muchowska et al. (2019) and Ritson (2021, Science Advances, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981). The reverse photooxidation is thermodynamically driven by UV absorption and is consistent with the photoredox framework of Liu et al. (2021, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00789-w). TiO2-mediated alcohol-to-aldehyde photooxidation is well-established in photocatalysis literature.

reactions.name: Formaldehyde Cannizzaro disproportionation to glycolic acid

conditions.other: Submarine hydrothermal system; 2 equivalents of formaldehyde required; alkaline conditions favor Cannizzaro

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo Cannizzaro-type disproportionation: one formaldehyde is oxidized (to formic acid) while the other is reduced (to methanol), followed by aldol-type condensation to yield the C2 α-hydroxy acid (glycolic acid). Alternatively, the two formaldehydes undergo direct crossed-Cannizzaro/aldol coupling to produce glycolic acid. Magnetite provides the mineral base surface facilitating the disproportionation.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde Cannizzaro disproportionation to glycolic acid under submarine hydrothermal conditions was demonstrated by Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2). The previous attempt incorrectly used only one formaldehyde input — here both equivalents are explicitly represented.

reactions.name: Acetate UV photoredox to glycolic acid

conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pool; ~4 hour reaction time; photoredox radical mechanism

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation generates a methyl radical from acetate via homolytic C–C bond cleavage of the carboxyl. The radical is hydroxylated by reaction with water-derived OH radical (or direct insertion of an oxygen atom from water), forming glycolic acid (HOCH2-COOH). TiO2 acts as a semiconductor photocatalyst generating reactive radical species.
reactions.reasoning: UV-driven photoredox radical coupling of acetate with water to produce glycolic acid over ~4 hours was demonstrated by Liu, Wu, Kufner, Sasselov, Fischer, and Sutherland (2021, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00789-w). This provides a photochemical surface route from the simplest carboxylate to the C2 α-hydroxy acid.

reactions.name: Oxidation of acetate to peracetic acid

conditions.other: Submarine hydrothermal system; oxidation by pyrite surface-generated reactive oxygen species or H2O2 from FeS/H2S reactions

reactions.mechanism: Acetate (CH3-COOH) is oxidized to peracetic acid (CH3-CO-OOH) by transfer of an oxygen atom. In hydrothermal systems, reactive oxygen species (H2O2, OH•) can be generated by Fenton-type reactions involving Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ cycling on pyrite surfaces, or by reaction of H2S with dissolved oxidants at vent margins. These oxidants convert the carboxylic acid to the percarboxylic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Peracetic acid as a glycine precursor was proposed by Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2). The prebiotic formation of peracetic acid from acetate is acknowledged as an open question in the literature. The most plausible route involves oxidation by H2O2 generated via pyrite-mediated Fenton chemistry (Cohn et al., 2004) or by FeS + H2S reactions producing reactive sulfur-oxygen intermediates. This reaction has the lowest confidence in the network and represents a speculative but necessary connection.

reactions.name: Peracetic acid amination to glycine

conditions.other: Submarine hydrothermal system; elevated temperature promotes nucleophilic substitution

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia (NH3) acts as a nucleophile, attacking the carbonyl carbon of peracetic acid (CH3-CO-OOH). The peroxide (-OOH) serves as a leaving group, displaced by ammonia to form the C–N bond. The resulting amide-like intermediate rearranges (via oxidative insertion at the methyl C–H bond or Beckmann-type rearrangement) to yield glycine (H2N-CH2-COOH). Pyrite surfaces may facilitate this by activating the peroxide bond.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2) under simulated submarine hydrothermal vent conditions. The exact mechanism of C–N bond formation and rearrangement to glycine from peracetic acid is less well-characterized than other pathways, as noted in the literature review.

reactions.name: L-Serine retro-aldol thermal decomposition to glycine

conditions.other: Sustained hydrothermal temperatures; retro-aldol cleavage thermally favored

reactions.mechanism: The C–C bond between the α-carbon and the hydroxymethyl side chain of serine (H2N-CH(CH2OH)-COOH) is cleaved via retro-aldol reaction, releasing the side chain as formaldehyde (CH2O) and yielding glycine (H2N-CH2-COOH). This is the reverse of the aldol condensation that would form serine from glycine + formaldehyde. Under sustained hydrothermal conditions, the retro-aldol direction is kinetically favored.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally demonstrated by Bada, Shou, Man, and Schroeder (1978, Earth and Planetary Science Letters, DOI: 10.1016/0012-821X(78)90042-0). Serine thermal decomposition to glycine + formaldehyde is well-characterized kinetically. The formaldehyde co-product is recycled into the network as a hub molecule.

reactions.name: L-Threonine retro-aldol thermal decomposition to glycine

conditions.other: Sustained hydrothermal temperatures

reactions.mechanism: Retro-aldol-type cleavage of the C–C bond between the glycine moiety (Cα–CO) and the threonine side chain. The 2-carbon side chain fragment undergoes intramolecular cyclization to form ethylene oxide (oxirane, C2H4O) as the co-product, while the remaining C2 fragment is glycine. Pyrite surface may catalyze the C–C bond cleavage.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by both Vallentyne (1964, Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, DOI: 10.1016/0016-7037(64)90147-4) and Bada et al. (1978, DOI: 10.1016/0012-821X(78)90042-0). L-Threonine is marked as an exogenous starting material — threonine is found in carbonaceous chondrite meteorites (Pizzarello et al., 2006) and can also be produced by Strecker synthesis from 2-ketobutyrate analogs in hydrothermal systems. Its presence on early Earth is well-supported.

reactions.name: L-Asparagine hydrolytic decomposition to glycine

conditions.other: Deep-sea hydrothermal vent temperatures; multi-step hydrolytic decomposition; produces 2 equivalents of glycine per asparagine

reactions.mechanism: Multi-step hydrolytic decomposition of asparagine at high temperature: (1) hydrolysis of the side-chain amide (-CONH2) to aspartate (-COOH) with release of NH3; (2) decarboxylation and/or retro-aldol cleavage of the C4 aspartate backbone to yield two molecules of glycine. The net reaction: asparagine + H2O → 2 glycine (+ NH3 + CO2 as minor co-products). Mackinawite surfaces facilitate the hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Demonstrated by Qian, Engel, Macko, Carpenter, and Deming (1993, Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, DOI: 10.1016/0016-7037(93)90540-D). The stoichiometry of 2 glycine per asparagine is explicitly stated in the literature. L-Asparagine is an exogenous starting material — it is found in meteorites (Pizzarello et al., 2006) and can be produced by amination of oxaloacetate-derived precursors. Note: the output array shows mol_012 once, but the reaction produces 2 equivalents.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of L-serine from glycolaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite clay and sulfide mineral surfaces
conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic surface pool; Strecker condensation + nitrile hydrolysis in wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde (HOCH2-CHO) condenses with ammonia to form an aminoalcohol imine (HOCH2-CH=NH), which undergoes nucleophilic addition by cyanide (CN⁻) to yield 2-amino-3-hydroxypropanenitrile (serinonitrile). Subsequent hydrolysis of the nitrile group in the wet phase of a wet-dry cycle produces L-serine (H2N-CH(CH2OH)-COOH). This is the C3 analog of the aminoacetonitrile → glycine Strecker pathway, using glycolaldehyde instead of formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Strecker synthesis of serine from glycolaldehyde, HCN, and NH3 is a well-established prebiotic route to this C3 amino acid (Miller, 1957; Peltzer et al., 1984). This provides a non-circular upstream source for serine, avoiding the circular dependency (glycine → serine → glycine) flagged in previous attempts. Glycolaldehyde is produced by the formose reaction (rxn_017), establishing a complete pathway from formaldehyde through glycolaldehyde to serine to glycine.

reactions.name: Formaldehyde aldol self-condensation to glycolaldehyde (formose)

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite clay with borate; Ca(OH)2 as base
conditions.other: Formose reaction first step; alkaline conditions; borate minerals stabilize products

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde (CH2O) undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation: one formaldehyde acts as the enolizable donor (umpolung via Cannizzaro intermediate or thiazolium-mediated pathway) and the other as the electrophilic acceptor, forming glycolaldehyde (HOCH2-CHO), the simplest sugar. Borate minerals stabilize the product by forming borate esters with the diol functionality, preventing further uncontrolled polymerization.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction, first described by Butlerow (1861), produces glycolaldehyde as the first C2 sugar from formaldehyde. Borate stabilization of sugars was demonstrated by Ricardo et al. (2004, Science). The formose reaction under prebiotic conditions on clay and borate mineral surfaces is reviewed by Benner et al. (2012). This reaction connects the C1 hub (formaldehyde) to the C2 sugar pool.

reactions.name: Reduction of nitric oxide to hydroxylamine by metallic iron

conditions.other: Anoxic hydrothermal system; NO from volcanic degassing or lightning-driven atmospheric chemistry; Fe0 from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: Metallic iron (Fe⁰) reduces nitric oxide (NO) in aqueous solution by electron transfer: Fe⁰ donates electrons, reducing NO stepwise to HNO and then to NH2OH (hydroxylamine). The iron is oxidized to Fe²⁺ in the process: Fe⁰ + NO + H2O → NH2OH + Fe²⁺ (simplified). This is analogous to industrial iron-mediated NOx reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Hydroxylamine production from NO reduction by Fe⁰ is the most prebiotically plausible source of this nitrogen donor. The literature review (Muchowska et al., 2019) notes hydroxylamine's prebiotic availability is debated but suggests it could form from NO reduction by Fe⁰. NO is produced by lightning discharge in early Earth's N2/CO2 atmosphere (Navarro-González et al., 2001) and by volcanic emissions. Fe⁰ is generated by serpentinization. This reaction addresses the hydroxylamine orphan problem from previous attempts.

reactions.name: UV photoredox synthesis of 2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid from CO2

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 photocatalyst; sulfite/sulfide as electron donor
conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pool; CO2 + sulfite under UV; early Earth photoredox conditions

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation drives the photoredox reduction of CO2 in the presence of sulfite/bisulfite as sacrificial electron donor. Two equivalents of CO2 are reduced and coupled to form the C2 intermediate 2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid (glyoxylic acid hydrate, HO-CH(OH)-COOH). TiO2 semiconductor particles absorb UV photons and generate electron-hole pairs that drive the CO2 reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Liu, Wu, Kufner, Sasselov, Fischer, and Sutherland (2021, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00789-w) demonstrated UV-driven prebiotic photoredox synthesis from CO2 and sulfite, producing C2 intermediates including 2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid. This reaction provides the upstream source for glyoxylic acid hydrate, addressing the orphan molecule problem from previous attempts.

reactions.name: Peptide bond formation: glycine dimerization to diglycine

conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling; dehydration during dry phase drives peptide bond formation; clay surface concentrates and orients glycine molecules

reactions.mechanism: Two glycine molecules undergo condensation to form the peptide bond: the amino group (-NH2) of one glycine attacks the carboxyl carbon (-COOH) of the other, displacing water to form the amide bond (-CO-NH-), yielding diglycine (Gly-Gly). Clay surfaces (montmorillonite) catalyze this by concentrating amino acids via adsorption, correctly orienting molecules, and lowering the activation energy. The dry phase of wet-dry cycling removes water, driving the condensation equilibrium forward.
reactions.reasoning: Clay-catalyzed peptide bond formation is a cornerstone of prebiotic polymer chemistry, demonstrated by Ferris et al. (1996, Nature) and Rode (1999). Montmorillonite specifically catalyzes the oligomerization of amino acids including glycine. This reaction connects the glycine synthesis network to downstream biomolecule assembly, representing the Biochemical environment stage.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation → Surface Strecker synthesis
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to formaldehyde via Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite in hydrothermal vents. Formaldehyde is transported to surface pools where it undergoes Strecker condensation with HCN and NH3 (on montmorillonite clay) to form aminoacetonitrile, which hydrolyzes under cyanosulfidic conditions to yield glycine. This is the most direct C1-to-glycine route, crossing from Hydrothermal to Surface environments.

pathways.name: Isocitrate cleavage → Iron-catalyzed reductive amination
pathways.description: Isocitrate (from the iron-driven proto-TCA network) undergoes Fe²⁺-catalyzed retro-aldol cleavage to glyoxylate and succinate. Glyoxylate is then reductively aminated to glycine by hydroxylamine in the presence of Fe⁰/Fe²⁺. Both reactions occur under hydrothermal conditions at ~70°C. Hydroxylamine is generated from NO reduction by Fe⁰ (rxn_018).

pathways.name: UV photoredox glyoxylate synthesis → Iron reductive amination
pathways.description: CO2 is photoreduced to 2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid in UV-irradiated surface pools (Liu et al., 2021). UV-driven dehydration converts this to glyoxylate. Glyoxylate is then transported to hydrothermal mixing zones where Fe⁰/Fe²⁺-catalyzed reductive amination with hydroxylamine yields glycine. This is a Surface → Hydrothermal cross-environment pathway.

pathways.name: Formaldehyde → Glycolic acid → Glyoxylate → Glycine
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to formaldehyde in hydrothermal vents (rxn_001). Two formaldehyde molecules undergo Cannizzaro disproportionation to glycolic acid (rxn_009). Glycolic acid is transported to surface pools where UV photooxidation converts it to glyoxylate (rxn_008). Glyoxylate returns to hydrothermal zones for reductive amination to glycine (rxn_005). This pathway traverses all three environments: Hydrothermal → Hydrothermal → Surface → Hydrothermal.

pathways.name: Acetate UV photoredox → Glycolic acid → Glyoxylate → Glycine
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to acetate on greigite in hydrothermal vents (rxn_002). Acetate is transported to surface pools where UV photoredox chemistry (Liu et al., 2021) converts it to glycolic acid (rxn_010). Glycolic acid is UV-photooxidized to glyoxylate (rxn_008), which returns to hydrothermal zones for iron-catalyzed reductive amination to glycine (rxn_005). This demonstrates the full Hydrothermal → Surface → Surface → Hydrothermal environmental cycle.

pathways.name: Acetate → Peracetic acid → Glycine (hydrothermal amination)
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to acetate on greigite (rxn_002). Acetate is oxidized to peracetic acid by pyrite-mediated reactive oxygen species (rxn_011). Peracetic acid reacts with ammonia under hydrothermal conditions to form glycine (rxn_012). This entirely hydrothermal pathway is the least well-characterized mechanistically, with peracetic acid formation representing the weakest link.

pathways.name: Formaldehyde → Glycolaldehyde → Serine → Glycine (Strecker + retro-aldol)
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to formaldehyde in hydrothermal vents (rxn_001). Formaldehyde undergoes aldol self-condensation in surface pools to glycolaldehyde (rxn_017). Glycolaldehyde undergoes Strecker synthesis with HCN and NH3 to yield serine (rxn_016). Serine is transported to hydrothermal zones where retro-aldol thermal decomposition produces glycine + formaldehyde (rxn_013). This is a NON-CIRCULAR pathway: serine is generated from glycolaldehyde (not from glycine), avoiding the circular dependency flagged in previous attempts.

pathways.name: L-Threonine thermal decomposition to glycine
pathways.description: L-Threonine (exogenous, from meteoritic delivery or broader prebiotic synthesis networks) undergoes retro-aldol-type thermal decomposition at hydrothermal vent temperatures to yield glycine + ethylene oxide (Bada et al., 1978; Vallentyne, 1964). This pathway illustrates glycine as a thermodynamic sink for amino acid degradation: more complex amino acids are recycled to the simplest amino acid under sustained thermal stress.

pathways.name: L-Asparagine hydrolytic decomposition to glycine
pathways.description: L-Asparagine (exogenous, from meteoritic delivery) undergoes multi-step hydrolytic decomposition at deep-sea hydrothermal vent temperatures, producing 2 equivalents of glycine per asparagine molecule (Qian et al., 1993). This demonstrates that even C4 amino acids are degraded to glycine at extreme temperatures.

pathways.name: Glycine to diglycine (downstream assembly)
pathways.description: Glycine produced by any of the above pathways undergoes peptide bond formation on montmorillonite clay surfaces during wet-dry cycling in the Biochemical environment, yielding diglycine (Gly-Gly). This demonstrates the downstream utility of glycine as a building block for peptide assembly, connecting the synthesis network to the origin of proteins.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Classical Strecker pathway with strong literature support (Patel et al. 2015). Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to formaldehyde feeds surface Strecker condensation and hydrolysis. rxn_001 is a slight simplification (FTT usually goes CO2→CO→formaldehyde) but acceptable as a net reaction. The environmental flow from hydrothermal to surface is well-justified.
suggestions: Consider specifying mineral catalyst for rxn_001 (e.g., awaruite, magnetite)
Could note that cyanosulfidic conditions in rxn_004 may involve sulfide species

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strongly supported by Muchowska et al. 2019 for both isocitrate cleavage and reductive amination. rxn_018 (NO → hydroxylamine via Fe⁰) is plausible but the weakest link—lacks a specific literature citation for this exact reduction. The pathway correctly identifies Fe⁰/Fe²⁺ catalysis. All reactions are hydrothermal, which is coherent but lacks cross-environment flow.
suggestions: Provide a specific citation for NO reduction to hydroxylamine by Fe⁰

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good surface-to-hydrothermal flow via glyoxylate. rxn_019 is problematic: Liu et al. 2021 used CO2 + sulfite under UV, but the network omits sulfite as a required reductant, writing only CO2 + H2O → dihydroxyacetic acid. This is a significant simplification that weakens the chemical validity. rxn_007 (UV dehydration to glyoxylate) is well-supported.
suggestions: Add sulfite (or bisulfite) as a required reactant in rxn_019
Specify UV wavelength range for surface reactions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Elegant 5-step pathway from CO2 to glycine through the C2 metabolic axis. Good environmental transitions: hydrothermal (formaldehyde, Cannizzaro) → surface (UV photooxidation of glycolic acid) → hydrothermal (reductive amination). rxn_009 Cannizzaro disproportionation is supported by Aubrey et al. 2008. rxn_008 photooxidation is supported by Liu et al. 2021. The pathway nicely demonstrates convergence through glyoxylate.
suggestions: Specify mineral surface for Cannizzaro reaction in rxn_009
Consider whether glycolic acid photooxidation requires a specific photosensitizer

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good alternative pathway through the acetate hub. CO2 reduction to acetate on greigite (rxn_002) is supported by Preiner et al. and related work. UV photoredox of acetate to glycolic acid (rxn_010, Liu et al. 2021) is well-documented. The hydrothermal-surface-hydrothermal oscillation is realistic. Shares the rxn_018 hydroxylamine weakness with P2-P4.
suggestions: Specify greigite (Fe3S4) explicitly as mineral catalyst in rxn_002

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The weakest pathway due to rxn_011 (acetate → peracetic acid). The literature identifies peracetic acid formation as an open question—the mechanism for oxidizing acetate to peracetic acid under hydrothermal conditions is not well-characterized. While rxn_012 (peracetic acid + NH3 → glycine) is supported by Aubrey et al. 2008, the precursor formation step is speculative. No specific oxidant or mineral catalyst is given for the oxidation.
suggestions: Specify the oxidant for acetate → peracetic acid conversion
Consider whether H2O2 or other prebiotic oxidants could drive this reaction
Provide literature support for the specific oxidation mechanism

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Clever pathway that builds serine via Strecker from glycolaldehyde and then decomposes it to glycine via retro-aldol. rxn_017 (formose self-condensation to glycolaldehyde) is well-known. rxn_016 (Strecker of glycolaldehyde to serine) is a reasonable extension of Strecker chemistry though less directly cited for this substrate. rxn_013 (serine retro-aldol) is strongly supported by Bada et al. 1978. Good environmental flow: hydrothermal → surface → hydrothermal. Note that this pathway recycles formaldehyde (produced in rxn_013, consumed in rxn_001), creating an interesting feedback loop.
suggestions: Add mineral catalyst specification for rxn_017 (e.g., borate minerals for formose selectivity)
Cite specific literature for Strecker synthesis of serine from glycolaldehyde

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Simple and well-supported decomposition reaction (Bada et al. 1978, Vallentyne 1964). The main limitation is that L-threonine is listed as an exogenous starting material (meteoritic delivery, Pizzarello et al. 2006), which is scientifically reasonable but makes this more of a recycling pathway than a de novo synthesis route. Single-environment pathway (hydrothermal only).
suggestions: Consider adding an upstream synthesis route for threonine to strengthen the pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Supported by Qian et al. 1993. Similar to P8, relies on exogenous asparagine (meteoritic delivery). The stoichiometry issue is notable: the strategy text states asparagine yields 2 glycine (matching literature), but rxn_015 only shows one glycine molecule as product. The reaction node should explicitly show 2 equivalents of glycine output. Single-environment pathway.
suggestions: Fix stoichiometry: rxn_015 should produce 2 mol_012 (glycine)
Specify the temperature conditions more precisely

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Appropriate downstream extension showing glycine utilization. Peptide bond formation is thermodynamically unfavorable in bulk aqueous solution and requires activation (e.g., wet-dry cycling, mineral surfaces, carbonyl sulfide). The biochemical environment label is appropriate. The pathway demonstrates that glycine produced by the network can be assembled into peptides.
suggestions: Specify the activation mechanism for peptide bond formation (wet-dry cycling, mineral catalysis, COS activation)
Consider adding more downstream products (triglycine, etc.)

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent and comprehensive synthesis network for prebiotic glycine production. The network demonstrates strong topology with 23 molecules and 20 reactions organized into 10 pathways with 5 hub molecules and 5 convergence points. Key strengths: (1) Outstanding literature alignment—nearly every reaction is traceable to a specific published study (Muchowska et al. 2019, Patel et al. 2015, Liu et al. 2021, Aubrey et al. 2008, Bada et al. 1978, Qian et al. 1993, Vallentyne 1964). (2) Glyoxylate serves as an excellent convergence node with four independent upstream routes. (3) Good environmental interconnection with clear hydrothermal → surface → biochemical flow. (4) The network addresses previous critiques about orphan molecules and circularity. Key weaknesses: (1) rxn_019 omits sulfite as a required reductant for CO2 photoredox. (2) rxn_011 (acetate → peracetic acid) lacks mechanistic support and is the weakest reaction. (3) rxn_018 (NO → hydroxylamine) needs stronger citation. (4) rxn_015 has a stoichiometric error (should produce 2 glycine). (5) Some mineral agents could be more specific. Despite these issues, the network is well above the approval threshold with multiple independently viable pathways to the target.
